title: The Role of the Claustrum in Psychedelic-Induced Altered States
body:
The Role of the Claustrum in Psychedelic-Induced Altered States

When I first started exploring psychedelic therapy, I was fascinated by the way these substances seemed to dissolve the boundaries of self, bending perception and cognition beyond normal limits. But I never gave much thought to what part of the brain might be orchestrating that experience until I stumbled upon mentions of a tiny, thin sheet of neurons called the claustrum. It’s one of those brain regions you don't hear about every day - hidden deep, sandwiched between the insula and the putamen, slender but seemingly important. What struck me was how some scientists began speculating that this small structure might be the conductor of our conscious experience, the brain’s secret switchboard for awareness. Could it be that the claustrum plays a key role in the psychedelic state? If so, what does that mean for our understanding of consciousness and healing?

One night, during a mushroom session, I sensed a sudden collapse of the usual “me” that negotiates through the world. I wasn’t simply seeing or feeling differently - I was inside a different mode of awareness. Later, as I read about the claustrum’s dense connectivity, I wondered if this brain region was partly responsible for that collapse of self-boundaries. After all, the claustrum links to nearly every cortical area, suggesting it could help integrate the senses and thoughts into a coherent whole. Was the psychedelic experience a brief “unplugging” or alteration of claustrum activity?

That question led me down a rabbit hole of neuroscience, psychopharmacology, and firsthand reports. I found that the claustrum’s role is still largely mysterious but increasingly recognized as a key player in consciousness. Some researchers think it acts like a control hub, coordinating our awareness by binding information from different parts of the brain. Psychedelics, by altering this hub, might temporarily loosen the tightly knit integration we call selfhood, opening the door to different ways of perceiving and feeling.

I also discovered that the claustrum has been linked to sensory perception, attention, and even the feeling of “oneness” people describe under psychedelics. This made me think about my own experiences and those shared by others: the sense of unity, the loss of ego boundaries, the vivid sensory amplification. Could these all tie back to the little-known claustrum?

So, this article explores what the claustrum is, what science says about its function, and how it might be implicated in the psychedelic state. Also, I’ll share practical thoughts on how understanding this could enrich your own journeys and integration work. Before we dive into the research, think about your own psychedelic experiences - have you noticed moments when your sense of self seemed to dissolve or your perception felt unusually unified? That feeling might be the claustrum at work.

Understanding the Claustrum: A Core Concept

The claustrum is a skinny, irregular sheet of gray matter embedded deep inside the brain. It’s often overshadowed by its neighbors - the insula, the putamen, the external capsule - but its unique anatomy and connectivity make it a fascinating subject. Neuroscientists describe it as one of the most densely connected regions in the brain, with projections to almost every cortical area. The sheer number of connections hints that it might serve as an integration hub, binding together sensory input, emotions, and higher cognitive functions into a unified experience.

Francis Crick and Christof Koch, two giants in neuroscience, once proposed the claustrum as a candidate for the neural basis of consciousness - a “conductor of the orchestra” that synchronizes different brain regions to create a seamless flow of awareness. Although this idea is still debated, it brought the claustrum into the spotlight for psychedelic researchers.

Functionally, the claustrum has been implicated in several key processes: attention, sensory integration, and conscious perception. Imaging studies show it activates when we shift attention, suggesting it helps focus our awareness on particular stimuli. It also seems involved in sensory binding - how the brain merges sights, sounds, and sensations into a coherent picture of reality. When intact, the claustrum might maintain the boundaries of self and environment, making sure the “I” stays distinct from the “other.”

Now, psychedelics such as psilocybin and LSD are known to disrupt the brain’s usual connectivity patterns. Carhart-Harris and colleagues have shown that these substances reduce activity in the default mode network (DMN), a set of brain regions involved in self-referential processing. But the claustrum’s role is less understood. Some evidence suggests psychedelics may reduce the functional coupling of the claustrum with other parts of the brain, loosening the tight control it exerts. This could explain the ego dissolution and altered sensory experiences common to psychedelic trips.

Interestingly, the claustrum is rich in serotonin 2A receptors - the primary target of classic psychedelics. This receptor density supports the idea that psychedelics directly act on the claustrum, affecting its ability to coordinate brain activity. When these receptors are activated, the claustrum's usual integration of sensory and cognitive information might falter, opening up the mind to novel connections and feelings.

So, the claustrum could be viewed as a gatekeeper of consciousness and selfhood, and psychedelics might temporarily loosen that gate, allowing the boundaries of the self to expand or dissolve. Understanding this core idea helps frame the psychedelic experience in terms of brain function, not just mystical language. It also suggests new ways to approach therapeutic use and integration.

What the Research Tells Us About Psychedelics and the Claustrum

Scientific studies directly examining the claustrum’s role in psychedelic experiences are still rare but growing. One notable paper by Kaelen et al. (2018) used fMRI to show that psilocybin reduces the functional connectivity between the claustrum and several cortical regions. This reduction correlated with subjective reports of ego dissolution - when users describe losing the feeling of being a separate self. That study was a landmark in linking claustrum activity with altered states.

Another fascinating study by Krimmel et al. (2019) explored how electrical stimulation of the claustrum in epilepsy patients caused temporary lapses in consciousness. This finding supports the idea that the claustrum is crucial for maintaining coherent awareness. If psychedelics disrupt claustrum activity, it makes sense that they cause deep changes in consciousness.

Research from the Carhart-Harris lab has mostly focused on the default mode network (DMN), but some of their data indirectly suggest claustrum involvement. For example, in their 2016 psilocybin study, they found a breakdown in the typical hierarchical organization of brain networks. Since the claustrum sits at an intersection of many networks, it could be a key node affected during the psychedelic state.

We also have animal studies that provide clues. In rodent models, activating serotonin 2A receptors in claustrum neurons changes firing patterns and disrupts normal oscillations, which might mirror the human psychedelic state. However, extrapolating from animals to humans is always tricky, especially with something as subjective as altered consciousness.

Several scholars like Robin Carhart-Harris and Roland Griffiths have emphasized the importance of understanding brain circuits beyond the DMN to fully explain psychedelic effects. The claustrum, with its vast connections, fits this criterion perfectly. Michael Pollan, in his book How to Change Your Mind, touches on these emerging ideas, encouraging readers to think about the brain’s architecture in new ways.

Despite the exciting findings, the claustrum remains somewhat mysterious because it is hard to isolate with standard imaging techniques. Its thin shape and location make it difficult to study. That said, newer imaging methods and computational models promise to shed more light in coming years.

One thing is clear: the claustrum is a promising piece of the psychedelic puzzle. Understanding its involvement may help us better map the neural basis of mystical experiences, ego dissolution, and the therapeutic potential of psychedelics.

How You Can Work with Claustrum-Related Insights in Your Psychedelic Practice

Knowing about the claustrum’s role offers more than just geeky neuroscience trivia. It can shape how you approach your psychedelic journeys and the integration afterward. For starters, recognizing that ego dissolution has a neurobiological basis might help normalize the frightening or disorienting moments when the “self” seems to vanish. Instead of fearing loss of control, you can understand this as a temporary shift in brain function, one that might open new doors in healing and insight.

During preparation for a session, keeping the claustrum in mind encourages you to focus on sensory awareness and attention. Since the claustrum helps bind sensory input, practices like mindful breathing, body scans, or gentle music can support a smoother journey by gently guiding the senses without overwhelming them. This can reduce the chance of getting lost in chaos or overwhelming sensory input.

Integration is where knowledge about the claustrum can truly shine. Many people feel the aftereffects of altered sensory and cognitive processing linger days or weeks after a session. Writing down your experiences in a journal can help remind your brain of the new connections it made while the claustrum’s usual “filtering” was loosened. I highly recommend a Journaling Notebook Leather Bound for capturing these impressions. Putting words to your experience re-establishes a coherent narrative self, helping to ground life-changing insights.

For those struggling with trauma or PTSD, the claustrum’s role in sensory integration is especially relevant. Bessel van der Kolk’s The Body Keeps the Score explains how trauma disrupts how the brain processes sensory and emotional information. Psychedelics, by modulating claustrum function, might help reset these patterns, allowing trauma to be accessed and integrated without retraumatization.

On a practical note, avoid overstimulation during sessions. The claustrum works hard to keep everything coherent, and overwhelming your senses with too much noise, light, or input can fragment the experience and increase anxiety. Creating a calm, safe environment respects the claustrum’s fragile balance and can lead to smoother, more meaningful trips.

Finally, patience is key. Changes in brain connectivity don’t instantly become permanent transformations. Repeated practice with conscious reflection, mindfulness, and therapeutic support helps the brain integrate what the claustrum and psychedelics opened up.

Common Challenges Linked to Claustrum Dysregulation and How to move through Them

Many people report challenges during or after psychedelic sessions that might relate to the claustrum’s altered state. For example, some experience overwhelming sensory input, confusion, or a terrifying loss of self. These can be traced to the claustrum struggling to bind sensory information and maintain coherent awareness when its normal function is disrupted.

One common issue is sensory overload. With the claustrum’s filtering loosened, every sound, color, and feeling can come in simultaneously, unfiltered. This can feel like your brain is exploding with raw data, making it hard to focus or find calm. From my own experience, grounding techniques - touching a textured object, feeling your feet on the floor, or focusing on steady breathing - can help the claustrum regain some control, anchoring awareness in the body.

Another challenge is ego fragmentation or depersonalization, where the sense of “I” shatters beyond what is comfortable. In this state, panic or confusion can arise because the brain isn’t used to functioning without its usual integrative hub. Holding space with a trusted guide or sitter, reassuring yourself that this is a temporary effect of altered brain activity, can help. When possible, gently redirecting attention to simple sensory inputs or recalling comforting memories can ease the crisis.

Post-session, some people experience lingering cognitive fuzziness or mood swings. This can reflect the claustrum and related networks re-stabilizing after being “shaken up.” Integration tools like journaling, therapy, and mindfulness meditation support the reshaping of new neural pathways. Keeping a consistent routine and avoiding substance misuse also help the brain settle back.

It’s important to recognize when claustrum-related dysregulation overlaps with mental health conditions. If you experience persistent psychosis, anxiety, or depression after psychedelics, professional support from therapists experienced in psychedelic integration is crucial. Researchers like Stanislav Grof have long warned that these altered states can unearth latent issues that need careful guidance.

One last tip: don’t fight the experience aggressively. The claustrum’s disruption can feel uncomfortable, but resisting it often amplifies distress. Instead, I’ve found that a gentle, curious attitude - acknowledging sensations and feelings without judgment - allows the brain’s natural healing processes to emerge. Sometimes, just sitting with the discomfort and breathing through it leads to deep breakthroughs.

Integrating Claustrum Insights into Your Daily Life

What happens after your psychedelic session is where the real work begins. Integration is not just about “making sense” of your journey but about weaving new brain patterns into everyday living. Since the claustrum influences how we organize sensory input and maintain self-awareness, supporting its balanced function can improve mental clarity, emotional regulation, and overall well-being long-term.

Daily mindfulness practices help maintain a calm and attentive claustrum. Even five minutes a day of sitting quietly, focusing on breath or bodily sensations, reinforces sensory binding and attentional control. I personally use a journaling habit to track subtle shifts in mood and perception, which I log in my Journaling Notebook Leather Bound. Writing anchors my awareness and trains the brain to hold new patterns gently.

Movement and bodywork are also excellent for supporting the claustrum’s sensory integration. Yoga, dance, or even simple walks with attention to the body’s sensations reinforce the natural flow of information between body and brain. This can be especially helpful when working through trauma, a point Bessel van der Kolk explores deeply in The Body Keeps the Score. Psychedelics may loosen claustrum activity, but long-term healing depends on helping the brain learn new organization through sensory experience.

Social connection also matters. The claustrum’s role in binding awareness suggests that shared experiences can help stabilize a sense of self and reality. Talking openly with trusted friends or support groups about your psychedelic experiences can reinforce coherent self-narratives and lessen isolation.

Finally, patience with yourself is vital. The brain’s networks don’t reset overnight. My own journey has taught me that integration is a slow unfolding. Sometimes the insights from a session surface weeks later, triggered by a conversation or a quiet moment. Respecting the pace at which your claustrum and brain recalibrate helps avoid frustration.

Remember, the claustrum’s delicate choreography sustains the “you” in everyday life. Caring for your brain’s health with balanced sleep, nutrition, hydration, and gentle mental challenges supports this complex dance.

A Reflection to Hold On To

Contemplating the claustrum’s role in psychedelic states lingers long after the data and theories have settled. It nudges us toward a humbling realization: our sense of self, that solid “I” behind our thoughts and feelings, might be less fixed than we imagine. The claustrum, a small, unassuming structure deep in the brain, acts like a fragile thread weaving together our experience of being. Psychedelics tug at that thread, loosening it just enough to reveal the spaciousness beneath the usual fabric.

This opens an intriguing possibility: what if the boundaries we call “self” are more like a dance of neural activity rather than a permanent entity? What if healing and transformation involve gently unbinding old patterns and reweaving new ones that are more fluid, flexible, and open? The claustrum’s central role in binding awareness offers a physical touchpoint for these abstract, deeply human questions.

As I write this, I’m reminded of the words of Grof, who described psychedelic states as visits to “the core of the psyche,” where the familiar self melts away, exposing something raw and authentic. The claustrum might be the neural stage on which this drama unfolds. It’s a reminder that our brain, in all its complexity, is still a living, breathing mystery.

So, as you move forward in your own psychedelic or contemplative practice, consider the claustrum’s whisper beneath the surface. It invites you to loosen the grip on rigid identity, explore the fluid nature of consciousness, and embrace the unfolding journey of becoming. It is not about losing yourself but finding new ways to be alive in the vast theater of experience.

And if you ever find yourself overwhelmed or lost in the vastness of a psychedelic trip, remember the claustrum’s quiet work - binding, holding, and sometimes letting go - as part of the complex dance that makes you, you.
